data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Saab (OM:SAAB B) After Its Strong Multi‑Year Rally?
data.description:
• Investors may be wondering if Saab's share price still offers value after a big run, or if most of the opportunity is already reflected in the current price.
  • The stock closed at SEK 565.0, with returns of 33.1% over the last year and a very large gain over 5 years. More recently it has seen a 5.1% decline over 7 days and an 11.0% decline over 30 days, while sitting around 0.9% year to date.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Saab's position within the Aerospace & Defense space and how investors are reacting to sector themes and contract flows. This helps explain why the share price has moved sharply at times. For you as an investor, the key question is whether current expectations built into the price are still reasonable.
  • Simply Wall St's valuation model gives Saab a value score of 0 out of 6. The next sections will unpack what that means across different valuation approaches and then finish with a practical way to think about whether the current price aligns with your own view of fair value.

Saab scores just 0/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Approach 1: Saab Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model looks at the cash Saab is expected to generate in the future and discounts those cash flows back to today to estimate what the business could be worth right now.

For Saab, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month free cash flow is reported at SEK 5,849.83m. Analyst estimates and Simply Wall St extrapolations point to projected free cash flows ranging from SEK 3,680m in 2026 to SEK 4,909.20m in 2035, all in SEK.

When these projected cash flows are discounted back to today and combined with an assumed terminal value, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of SEK 193.11 per share. Compared with the recent share price of SEK 565.0, the DCF output implies that the stock is 192.6% above this intrinsic value estimate, which signals a rich valuation on this specific cash flow based approach.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Saab may be overvalued by 192.6%. Discover 231 high quality undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Saab.

Approach 2: Saab Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to connect what you pay for each share with the earnings that back it. It helps you see how many years of current earnings the market is effectively pricing into the stock.

What counts as a "normal" P/E depends a lot on growth expectations and risk. Higher expected earnings growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually call for a lower one.

Saab currently trades on a P/E of 46.87x. That sits below the Aerospace & Defense industry average of 51.21x, but above the peer average of 40.64x. To go a step further, Simply Wall St calculates a proprietary “Fair Ratio” of 45.16x for Saab, which is the P/E that might be expected given factors such as its earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and specific risks.

This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a simple comparison with peers or the industry, because it adjusts for Saab’s own characteristics rather than assuming all companies should trade at similar multiples.

Comparing the Fair Ratio of 45.16x with the actual P/E of 46.87x suggests Saab is trading above that reference level, so the shares appear overvalued on this earnings multiple basis.

Result: OVERVALUED

Approach 1: AST SpaceMobile Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes projected future cash flows, then discounts them back to today to estimate what the business might be worth now. It is essentially asking what those future dollars are worth in today’s money.

For AST SpaceMobile, the latest twelve month free cash flow (FCF) is a loss of $1,438.9m, so the model starts from a deeply negative cash position. Analysts have provided FCF estimates out to 2030, with Simply Wall St extrapolating further to build a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model. Within this, annual projections move from losses in 2026 and 2027 into positive FCF by 2029, reaching $975.6m in 2030, all in $.

When all these projected cash flows are discounted back, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $133 per share. Against a recent share price of $76.40, this implies the stock is 42.6% undervalued on these assumptions.

Investors usually accept a higher or lower price to book ratio depending on growth expectations, quality of assets and perceived risk, so there is no single “right” level across all businesses.

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth today by projecting the cash it may generate in the future and discounting those cash flows back to a present value.

For Union Pacific, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $5.80b. Analyst and extrapolated projections suggest Free Cash Flow reaching around $10.31b by 2030, with interim yearly projections between 2026 and 2035 discounted back to today to build up the valuation.

On this basis, the DCF model points to an estimated intrinsic value of about $327.03 per share. Against a recent share price around $268.70, this implies a 17.8% discount, which indicates that the stock appears undervalued on this model.

Itron currently trades on a P/E of 13.04x, compared with an Electronic industry average of 27.39x and a peer group average of 78.72x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Itron is 19.59x. This Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what the P/E could be, based on factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks. Because it adjusts for these fundamentals, it can be more tailored than a simple comparison against industry or peer averages.

Set against this Fair Ratio, Itron’s current P/E of 13.04x sits below the 19.59x level.
